item.title: Compass Therapeutics Biliary Tract Cancer Bispecific
item.description:
• Summary: Compass Therapeutics reported topline results from a pivotal Phase 3 trial of its bispecific antibody (CTX-009) targeting DLL4 and VEGF in biliary tract cancer. The trial missed the primary endpoint of overall survival, with confounding data raising questions about approvability. Shares plunged as investors digested the mixed results. Secondary endpoint analyses showed a potential benefit in a subset of patients, but the regulatory path remains uncertain.
  • MoAs: Bispecific antibody simultaneously inhibits DLL4 (Notch pathway) and VEGF (angiogenesis) signaling, disrupting tumor angiogenesis and growth.
  • Sources: Fierce Biotech, BioSpace, ApexOnco, Endpoints News

item.title: Erasca RAS Cancer Drug
item.description:
• Summary: Erasca released data from a study of its RAS pathway inhibitor (ERAS-007) in pancreatic cancer, showing some efficacy signals. However, the announcement was overshadowed by a legal threat from Revolution Medicines, which claimed patent infringement over efficacy assertions. The dispute could complicate Erasca's progress in a competitive RAS-targeted landscape.
  • MoAs: Inhibitor of the RAS signaling pathway (e.g., ERK kinase inhibitor), blocking downstream proliferation and survival signals in KRAS-mutant cancers.
  • Sources: Endpoints News, STAT News, Endpoints News

item.title: Relay Therapeutics Atirmociclib
item.description:
• Summary: Relay Therapeutics is advancing atirmociclib, an oral CDK2 inhibitor, into later-stage development for HR+/HER2- breast cancer with CCNE1 amplification. The company announced updated Phase 1/2 data showing promising monotherapy activity and a manageable safety profile. Atirmociclib is designed to be selective over CDK4/6, potentially offering a differentiated treatment option in a crowded CDK inhibitor landscape. Relay plans to initiate a pivotal study pending regulatory feedback.
  • MoAs: Atirmociclib is a selective inhibitor of cyclin-dependent kinase 2 (CDK2), blocking cell cycle progression in tumors with CCNE1 amplification or cyclin E overexpression.
  • Sources: Oncology Pipeline
